metadata.title: Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade: Public Health Implications and Policy Insights

abstract:
The Supreme Court of the United States' (SCOTUS) decision in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization overturned the Court's previous decision in Roe v. Wade, the case that established abortion before fetal viability as a constitutionally protected right in the USA, thereby returning abortion legality prior to viability back to individual states. Social media reactions indicated strong rebukes of SCOTUS' actions, potentially indicating support to maintain legal abortion to some extent. Yet, understanding the nuances in public opinion about abortion at the population scale is lacking. Objective To measure reactions to overturning Roe v. Wade (an outcome of the Dobbs v. Jackson decision) via social media. Methods We applied Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic models to generate themes for tweets (N = 4,353,103) collected between 5-3-2022 and 7-6-2022. We then applied a Sentence Bi-directional Encoder from Transformers (S-BERT) analysis with a restricted sample to evaluate daily themes and longitudinal changes. Finally, we applied a sentiment analysis to measure affect and changes in daily posting volume. Results Social media reactions to overturning Roe v. Wade were largely negative. LDA and S-BERT topics indicated mostly unease at SCOTUS' actions and changes to abortion access, though we also observe some support for SCOTUS and its decision. Increased abortion-related social media reactions lasted approximately 7 days before returning to baseline levels.

Although initial reactions to overturning Roe v. Wade were in support of abortion, rapid news cycles may have diverted attention away from this monumental issue. Declining coverage about Dobbs, and overturning Roe v. Wade, and by extension social media reactions, holds serious implications for other social issues that will likely be heard before SCOTUS in coming terms. Our findings can inform US abortion climate and promote awareness of the tenuous nature of US social issues and associated rights.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
paragraphs: The Supreme Court of the United States' (SCOTUS) ruling in Dobbs v. Jackson Women's Health Organization (hereafter referred to as Dobbs v. Jackson), on June 24, 2022, ended a 49-year precedent establishing abortion before viability as a constitutionally protected right in the US (Roe v. Wade, 410 US 113;Foster, 2022). In a court document leaked to the public by Politico, on May 2, 2022, Justice Alito argued that the initial ruling in Roe v. Wade was, "egregiously wrong from the start"-and that it was within the purview of SCO-TUS to re-evaluate the national position on abortion and correct for potential unconstitutionality that was incurred by that decision (and subsequent decisions regarding abortion) via Dobbs v. Jackson (Gerstein & Ward, n.d.), the case heard by SCOTUS challenging a 2018 Mississippi law banning abortion after 15 weeks of gestation "except in a medical emergency or in the case of a severe fetal abnormality." (Mississippi Legislature, 2017Legislature, , 2018)).

Though this is not the first time SCOTUS has reversed a landmark decision (see the Lochner era or Prohibition years) (Beaney, 1962;Siegel, 1991), the decision to overturn Roe v. Wade, and return legal purview over abortion to individual states is perhaps particularly jarring given the national climate and socially complex views regarding abortion (Jozkowski et al., 2021). This action also reflects a critical junction in which a previously established federally protected rightaccess to safe and legal abortions-was overturned. Indeed, the majority of US adults endorse legal abortion in the context of health (e.g., if the pregnant person's life/health may be endangered) or personal autonomy in conjunction with healthcare provider input (Bowman & Goldstein, 2021). According to trend data from Pew Research Center and Gallup, upwards of 60% of US adults support upholding Roe v. Wade (Bowman & Goldstein, 2021;Crawford et al., 2021). Furthermore, Pew reports that a majority of US adults (61%) endorse legal abortion in most, but not all, cases (Pew, 2022). This trend has remained consistent since 1973, with marginal variation over time (Bowman & Goldstein, 2021).

The broad endorsement for upholding Roe v. Wade, which itself represents a compromised approach for some to abortion legality given the trimester framework used in the decision (for more information on the decision see US Reports: Roe v. Wade) (Library of Congress, 1973), as well as the mixed support for legal abortion based on circumstance (Jozkowski et al., 2018), underscores the contextual and nuanced nature of people's attitudes toward abortion in the US. Indeed, embedded within abortion attitudes are beliefs regarding morality, ethics, and social determinants (Valdez et al., 2022). These sometimes competing dimensions, suggest that abortion attitudes are not static or polar, but contextual, sometimes contradictory, and complex (Jozkowski et al., 2021). Social attitude research on abortion suggests personal positions about abortion are not reflected in dichotomous "pro-choice" or "pro-life" categories but are mired with cooccurring factors that influence opinion (John, 1987). Such complexity makes articulating beliefs about the legal, social, medical, and ethical aspects of abortion challenging. Access to user generated content, such as social media, can potentially aid in understanding people's beliefs about abortion.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
sections.header_raw: Social Media Data as an Indicator of Public Sentiment Toward Abortion
sections.header_norm: social media data as an indicator of public sentiment toward abortion
paragraphs: Prior to the internet, news and current events were principally consumed via broadcast and print media (Sagan & Leighton, 2010). These traditional forms of media consumption implied that consumers received content at fixed intervals, including when newspapers hit print and hours in which local and national news were telecast. Social media's advent in the internet era accompanied changes in how people consume news and with what frequency (Newman, 2011). Boczkowski et al. (2017) refer to this change as incidental news consumption and information overload, where people, primarily young adults, constantly receive and passively review a barrage of news through their social media feeds. Rapid news sharing has thusly created 24-hour news cycles (Rosenberg & Feldman, 2008) where current estimates indicate interest in typical news stories endure for seven days before the public becomes disengaged and shifts focus to a new topic or emerging news cycle (Castillo et al., 2014).

A consequence of incidental news consumption is the tendency to comment on the issue via social media, which often contain opinions in support or against major national events. Predictably, the aftermath of the Dobbs v. Jackson ruling leak, and subsequent overturn by SCOTUS 6 weeks later, on June 24, 2022, saw exponential increases in social media activity related to abortion and Roe v. Wade. Perspectives on abortion presented on social media provide additional insight for understanding the national abortion climate, at least among people who use social media to comment on news and national events. Although not everyone leverages social media for information sharing purposes, current estimates indicate that at least 70% of the US adult population uses at least one social media platform daily, and among social media users, about half report receiving and engaging with news as a primary use for individual platforms (Atske, 2021). Strong engagement with social media as a news source suggests mining social media for insights into public opinion may reveal nuanced aspects of the abortion debate that may not be as apparent in traditional media, such as broadcast news and radio. Valdez et al. (2021) contend that social media data can serve as either exploratory insight into phenomena or to support findings derived from polls and surveys. Social media data also provide insights into free-speech given that these data, especially on Twitter (prior to its takeover by current owner, Elon Musk), often contain deeper exchanges across users that may implicate people's opinions, scope of opinions, and accuracy of opinions relative to accurate facts and information. Previous studies leveraging social media data for social issues, including COVID-19 (Valdez et al., 2020) and the aftermath of George Floyd's death (Bathina et al., 2021b), have captured rich, nuanced, and in-the-moment portrayals of the event as a major news cycle. Social media data have also been previously leveraged to study public reaction and implications for public policy (see trans sports bans (Pickett et al., 2019) and COVID-19 vaccine mandates (Bento et al., 2020;Shahsavari et al., 2020)). Given the monumental scope of the Dobbs v. Jackson decision, insights into climate, reactions, and engagement over time are strongly warranted especially given our societies governance and overreliance on technology.

sections.header_raw: Natural Language Processing: a Brief Primer in Social Media Analysis
sections.header_norm: natural language processing a brief primer in social media analysis
paragraphs: In comparison with surveys or other forms of primary data collection, social media data is abundant and, in most circumstances, instantly accessible via several outlets, including Application Programming Interfaces (APIs) and thirdparty web applications that scrape these data for a fee (Taylor & Pagliari, 2018). These APIs and third-party applications can be programmed using computer code to collect and store social media posts and associated metadata, including text, images, hyperlinks, and engagement metrics, by using queries to elicit posts spanning certain topics. Importantly, the ability to strategically query for events, phrases, or hashtags also creates comprehensive datasets around a fixed issue or sets of issues. However, an effective analysis of social media data necessitates strategies to reduce the burden of overly abundant data.

Advances in natural language processing (NLP) and social media mining have offered a window into public reactions to various public health and social issues (Guandong, 2013;Valdez, 2018). These computer science tools allow us to surveil population-wide reactions to major societal events with public health implications (Bollen et al., 2011). Tools associated with NLP, which perform a variety of tasks from theme generation to mood approximation, are born out of computational linguistics. Computational linguistics is a field of study that intends to program machines and computers to contextualize, understand, and, to an increasing extent, mimic natural human language (Grishman, 1986).

There are two broad categories of NLP methods: unsupervised and supervised. The distinguishing factor between the two is whether input data, such as social media content, is compared against other pre-trained language data. Unsupervised analyses do not involve this comparison, which means unsupervised analyses are entirely exploratory. Although there is value in such exploratory analyses, output derived from these tools may be slightly overgeneralized and, at times, unclear (Vlachos, 2011). In contrast, supervised analyses can contextualize raw output through the pre-trained data comparison (Nasteski, 2017). These analyses are more effective at capturing nuances in the data, including pro, anti, or neutral sentiments. However, these analyses require substantial computing power, often beyond the capabilities of standard Random Access Memory (RAM), though cloudbased coding platforms, including Google Collaboratory, are making supervised analyses more accessible. Regardless of whether one uses an unsupervised or supervised NLP approach, the single most important predictor of clarity in analysis is a largely abundant data. In cases where news cycles are imperative enough to sweepingly capture public attention, these data can be modeled for insights into social reactions during this fixed time.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
paragraphs: This study characterizes social media reactions to the overruling of Roe v. Wade by SCOTUS. We specifically investigated posted content during a period spanning immediately after the initial Dobbs v. Jackson leak on May 2, 2022 (i.e., we started scraping the following morning on May 3, 2022), and past the subsequent ruling on June 24, 2022. Our study is guided by the following research questions:

RQ1. What themes emerge from a corpus of US tweets about Roe v. Wade during the SCOTUS leak and overturn? RQ2. Are there changes in posting volume related to abortion and the overturn of Roe v. Wade from data collected during the leak versus data collected during the decision announcement?

Findings from this study yield insights into populationlevel public opinion about Roe v. Wade among people who use social media. Findings also forecast the potential outcomes of other public health related issues decided on similar grounds to Roe v. Wade-namely the right to privacy. These issues remain at risk for restriction or overturn amid the SCOTUS' historical overturn of Roe v. Wade. We highlight important implications for public health and abortion as healthcare.

Findings also forecast the potential outcomes of other public health related issues decided on similar grounds to Roe v. Wade-namely the right to privacy. These issues remain at risk for restriction or overturn amid the SCOTUS' historical overturn of Roe v. Wade. We highlight important implications for public health and abortion as healthcare.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
paragraphs: Data for this study were collected in two waves from Twitter, a microblogging social networking website, prior to its privatization by current owner, Elon Musk. We collected the first wave, hereafter referred to as the "leak" corpus, between May 3 (the day following the leak) and May 17, 2022; and the second wave, hereafter referred to as the "overturn" corpus between June 22 (2 days prior to the Dobbs ruling) and July 6, 2022. We began collecting data for the "leak" corpus 1 day after the Politico leak (i.e., May 3rd) due to the surprise nature of the document leak-we were not able to collect immediately due to the time it took to program a streamer. Alternatively, we began collecting data 2 days prior to the official Dobbs ruling for the "overturn" corpus due to advance notice that the official decision by SCOTUS in Dobbs v. Jackson (which overturned Roe v. Wade) was imminent. Because we anticipated a strong spike in posting volume relating to abortion and Roe v. Wade, we continuously collected data for both corpus until we observed consistent posting volume for three consecutive days. Stated differently, we intended to capture any spikes in content related to abortion and Roe v. Wade, and measure how quickly content returned to stable or consistent levels. This period lasted 14 days for both corpora (leak corpus: May 3 through May 17, 2022; overturn corpus: June 22-July 6, 2022).

All data were procured by programming a live-streaming bot routed through Twitter's Application Programing Interface (API), which allows any Twitter user to query approximately 1% of total tweet volume per search (Colditz et al., 2018;Martini et al., 2021). To program a bot, we created a loop function in Python that routed queries continuously through the Twitter API. This results in data being captured as it is posted. In other words, as users tweeted about Roe v. Wade, approximately 1% of that data was captured by our bot. The bot then saves the tweet and associated metadata, such as likes, shares, and retweets, into a .csv file for further analysis.

For both the leak and overturn corpora, we simply collected tweets containing "Roe v. Wade" and associated variants, including using Roe v. Wade in the context of a hashtag (e.g., #RoevWade) or potential misspellings, which were denoted by an (*), which allows the streamer to collect any misspelled variants for Roe v. Wade (i.e., Row v Wade). We used "Roe v. Wade" as the sole search term for this study because "Roe v. Wade" is widely known and associated with abortion among US adults and the decision in Dobbs v. Jackson had direct implications for overturning this decision. We rationalized that people would be much more familiar with Roe v. Wade than Dobbs v. Jackson, and research supports this assumption (Jozkowski et al., 2023a). For more information of programming streamers and appropriate query selection for testable hypotheses, please see Zaki et al. (2020).

Our total sample yielded a robust dataset comprised of nLeak = 898,858 and nOverturn = 3,454,235 for a composite sample of N = 4,353,103 Twitter posts. Please note that our composite sample size greatly exceeds the mean observed in a meta-analysis of public health social media studies (n = 20,000) (Edo-Osagie et al., 2020). Our collection, use, and analyses of these data conform to Twitter's terms of service and ethical guidelines for social media research and secondary data analysis; they were deemed exempt by the Institutional Review Board.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
paragraphs: We leveraged several NLP methods divisible into two categories: (1) tools for theme generation, and (2) tools for mood approximation. For theme generation, we applied unsupervised (Latent Dirichlet Allocation (LDA) topic models) (Blei et al., 2003) and supervised (Sentence Bi-direction Encoder from Transformers (S-BERT)) (Roshanzamir et al., 2021) algorithms to identify themes and changes in themes over time. We used LDA, an unsupervised NLP tool that uses probability distributions and Gibbs sampling to identify and group co-occurring words into topics, to construct themes for the entirety of the leak and overturn corpora, inclusive of data that are known to cause noise such as quoted tweets and retweets. Because LDA can, at times, generate superficial topics, we increased the granularity of our analysis by examining only original tweets in both corpora with the supervised BERT algorithm. Original tweets are often sought for more granular analysis as this content is wholly original and authored by a single person, thereby reflecting personal opinions. Retweets, quote tweets, or shared tweets (which we included in the original LDA analysis) often contain content from 3rd parties, or news sources, which may detract from the clarity of models derived from more refined algorithms. Indeed, although retweets are vital and necessary to understand the scope of collected social media posts, to truly contextualize language, it is recommended they be removed so that reactions can be more organically traced to an original author and not be traced to a 3rd party or bot-authored account, though more research on this area is needed.

For the analysis of original tweets, we used an S-BERT pipeline with BERTopic, which uses a neural network approach trained with large-scale text data from which vectors for semantic representations are calculated and leveraged to generate personal reaction themes for original tweet data. To calculate vectors for each tweet, the BERT family generates a numeric vector value per tweet, which represents its semantics. This vector can be compared with other vectors using cosine similarity, which is used to gauge orthogonality (dissimilarity) to collinearity (perfect similarity). Because vectors generated by the BERT pipeline are highly complex, we apply a dimension reduction technique for interpretation purposes. Our pipeline used a combination of Uniform Manifold Approximation Projection (UMAP) to reduce of dimensions, and HDBScan, a density-based algorithm, to cluster our vectors into one of k clusters. We then applied a class-based Term-Frequency Inverse Document Frequency (cTF-IDF) to extract themes embedded within our clusters.

Lastly, to approximate mood, we applied a neural network sentiment analysis (Li et al., 2019), which predicts whether text can be classified along positive, negative, or neutral affect. Neural network sentiment analyses are trained on massive datasets to ensure accuracy and validity of each classification and are well-suited to evaluate social media data. For this analysis, we also used only original tweets as retweets, quote tweets, or shared tweets may not necessarily reflect the in-the-moment reactions posted by social media users about Roe v. Wade, our query, and by extension the Dobbs v. Jackson decision. Python modules specific to the data collection and analysis of this study include Tweepy, Pandas, Sentence Transformers (HuggingFace and Distillbert), BERTopic, NLTK, PYLDAVis, Matplotlib, Tensor-Flow; HDBScan, UMAP, and sklearn.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
paragraphs: Our workflow is depicted in Fig. 1. To prepare data for analyses, we first pre-processed all tweets in both corpora. This process involves removing redundant information (e.g., numbers, punctuation, or words such as is, it, or that), which allows the computer to tokenize words into numeric representations and vectors (Valdez et al., 2021). For our analyses, we first performed LDA topic models to ascertain very broad and general themes embedded within the leak and overturn corpora, inclusive of sources known in NLP studies to create noise (i.e., retweets and quote tweets) and account for overly popular social media posts throughout data collection. Then, two researchers reviewed topics and ascribed meaning; a common practice in unsupervised NLP analyses (Bathina et al., 2021a).

Following the completion of the LDA portion of the study, we then restricted our data to only include original tweets-original content posted by a single user that had not been previously shared or re-shared on Twitter. We generated several S-BERT models with BERTopic for our restricted leak and overturn datasets. This included a general analysis of all original tweets in both corpora, and a follow-up analysis of tweets generated by day to (1) generate daily themes, and (2) evaluate changes in themes over time.

We then ran sentiment analyses on the restricted datasets to evaluate affect among original tweets in both corpora. Sample size for our restricted data were nLeak(NoRT) = 60,145 and nOverturn(NoRT) = 202,218.
Our decision to run an LDA on the general dataset and S-BERT on original tweets is supported by literature.

First, numerous studies revere LDA as a gold-standard and highly adaptable tool for unsupervised NLP analyses (Anoop et al., 2015). LDA's computing requirements are also relatively small, which makes it possible to evaluate hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of documents without difficulty under the "bag of words" metaphor (Blei et al., 2003). However, LDA is limited in that the algorithm is not pre-trained on human language; as such, topics derived in the LDA model may be repetitive and/or redundant despite these topics representing core components of a corpus. By extension, S-BERT (and the larger BERT family) are supervised algorithms trained on human language (Ettinger, 2020). Leveraging contextual embeddings (by running text against BERT, S-BERT, or BERT topic and a pipeline of processing and clustering analyses) can capture the contextual nature of documents in a corpus. As such, topics derived from S-BERT analyses are typically more clear, less redundant, and more easily contextualized regarding content in the corpus (Edinger et al., 2022). However, calculating S-BERT or BERT vectors for a series of documents in a corpus requires substantial processing power, thereby limiting the number of documents for which sentence embeddings can be captured. In a contrast between various classes of topic models, Grootendorst (2022) supports the combined use of multiple topic model approaches based on research questions or anticipated outcomes. Other studies have used a similar split structure (i.e., two different approaches with the same or similar data), further supporting our use of LDA and S-BERT topic models (Bathina et al., 2021a, b;Valdez et al., 2020).

Wade Overturned (June 22-July 6, 2022) LDA Topic Models (All Data) LDA Topic Models (All Data) Remove all retweets Sen ment Analysis (All Data) Sentence BERT (By Day) Sentence BERT (All) Sen ment Analysis (All Data) Sentence BERT (By Day) Sentence BERT (All) Remove all retweets Fig. 1 Workflow and conceptual pipeline of analysis beginning with data collection via Twitter's Application Programming Interface (API) Results RQ1: What Themes Emerge from a Corpus of US Tweets About Roe v. Wade During the SCOTUS Leak and Overturn? LDA Topic Models for Leak and Overturn Corpora (All Tweets)

Our analyses revealed a multitude of possible topics embedded within both corpora. Though topics were diverse in content (see Table 1), we found a high degree of overlap in themes between corpora, reflecting consistent and unchanging discourse. Examples of repeated content in both discourses included opining against SCO-TUS actions and compelling people to vote Democrat in future elections. However, we also observed differences in topics between the leak and overturn corpora, which may have been indicative of news cycles at the point of data collection, including the possibility of Roe v. Wade being overturned (leak corpora) and certainty that Roe v. Wade had been overturned (overturn corpora). Indeed, during the leak we observed disbelief at the authenticity of the leak document (i.e., TWEET: I just saw that Roe v. Wade will be overturned…this is a joke, right?) However, post overturn, we observed more critical tweets outright condemning SCOTUS (i.e., TWEET: SCOTUS is COMPROMISED) and, in other cases, celebrating the end of Roe v. Wade (i.e., TWEET: Millions of unborn lives SAVED!!!!). Importantly, for both the leak and overturn corporaand as is common for LDA analyses-we observed several overlapping topics within the corpora, which is observed in several topics containing the same name. In the leak corpus, for example, we identified three topics specific to SCOTUS reactions, defined as any outright comment regarding SCOTUS as a court, or SCOTUS' collective actions, which was typically, although not always, in a negative context. When we examined the words specific to each of the three SCOTUS reaction topics, we concluded that each topic exhibited reactions to SCOTUS in different, almost mutually distinct contexts. For example, SCOTUS reaction topic one reflected tweets expressing anger at SCOUTS by encouraging people to vote for Democrats in future election; SCOTUS reaction topic two reflected disappointment in SCOTUS by calling to end the filibuster; and SCOTUS reaction topic three reflected comments directed at SCOTUS calling the court, and by extension the USA, a theocracy, or government by religion. In this way, while topics may share the same overarching name, they reflect different aspects of a similar theme, warranting the need for supervised NLP to contextualize topics.

Leveraging contextual embeddings (by running text against BERT, S-BERT, or BERT topic and a pipeline of processing and clustering analyses) can capture the contextual nature of documents in a corpus. As such, topics derived from S-BERT analyses are typically more clear, less redundant, and more easily contextualized regarding content in the corpus (Edinger et al., 2022). However, calculating S-BERT or BERT vectors for a series of documents in a corpus requires substantial processing power, thereby limiting the number of documents for which sentence embeddings can be captured. In a contrast between various classes of topic models, Grootendorst (2022) supports the combined use of multiple topic model approaches based on research questions or anticipated outcomes. Other studies have used a similar split structure (i.e., two different approaches with the same or similar data), further supporting our use of LDA and S-BERT topic models (Bathina et al., 2021a, b;Valdez et al., 2020).

Our analyses revealed a multitude of possible topics embedded within both corpora. Though topics were diverse in content (see Table 1), we found a high degree of overlap in themes between corpora, reflecting consistent and unchanging discourse. Examples of repeated content in both discourses included opining against SCO-TUS actions and compelling people to vote Democrat in future elections. However, we also observed differences in topics between the leak and overturn corpora, which may have been indicative of news cycles at the point of data collection, including the possibility of Roe v. Wade being overturned (leak corpora) and certainty that Roe v. Wade had been overturned (overturn corpora). Indeed, during the leak we observed disbelief at the authenticity of the leak document (i.e., TWEET: I just saw that Roe v. Wade will be overturned…this is a joke, right?) However, post overturn, we observed more critical tweets outright condemning SCOTUS (i.e., TWEET: SCOTUS is COMPROMISED) and, in other cases, celebrating the end of Roe v. Wade (i.e., TWEET: Millions of unborn lives SAVED!!!!). Importantly, for both the leak and overturn corporaand as is common for LDA analyses-we observed several overlapping topics within the corpora, which is observed in several topics containing the same name. In the leak corpus, for example, we identified three topics specific to SCOTUS reactions, defined as any outright comment regarding SCOTUS as a court, or SCOTUS' collective actions, which was typically, although not always, in a negative context. When we examined the words specific to each of the three SCOTUS reaction topics, we concluded that each topic exhibited reactions to SCOTUS in different, almost mutually distinct contexts. For example, SCOTUS reaction topic one reflected tweets expressing anger at SCOUTS by encouraging people to vote for Democrats in future election; SCOTUS reaction topic two reflected disappointment in SCOTUS by calling to end the filibuster; and SCOTUS reaction topic three reflected comments directed at SCOTUS calling the court, and by extension the USA, a theocracy, or government by religion. In this way, while topics may share the same overarching name, they reflect different aspects of a similar theme, warranting the need for supervised NLP to contextualize topics.

Our workflow is depicted in Fig. 1. To prepare data for analyses, we first pre-processed all tweets in both corpora. This process involves removing redundant information (e.g., numbers, punctuation, or words such as is, it, or that), which allows the computer to tokenize words into numeric representations and vectors (Valdez et al., 2021). For our analyses, we first performed LDA topic models to ascertain very broad and general themes embedded within the leak and overturn corpora, inclusive of sources known in NLP studies to create noise (i.e., retweets and quote tweets) and account for overly popular social media posts throughout data collection. Then, two researchers reviewed topics and ascribed meaning; a common practice in unsupervised NLP analyses (Bathina et al., 2021a).

Following the completion of the LDA portion of the study, we then restricted our data to only include original tweets-original content posted by a single user that had not been previously shared or re-shared on Twitter. We generated several S-BERT models with BERTopic for our restricted leak and overturn datasets. This included a general analysis of all original tweets in both corpora, and a follow-up analysis of tweets generated by day to (1) generate daily themes, and (2) evaluate changes in themes over time.

We then ran sentiment analyses on the restricted datasets to evaluate affect among original tweets in both corpora. Sample size for our restricted data were nLeak(NoRT) = 60,145 and nOverturn(NoRT) = 202,218.

Our decision to run an LDA on the general dataset and S-BERT on original tweets is supported by literature.

First, numerous studies revere LDA as a gold-standard and highly adaptable tool for unsupervised NLP analyses (Anoop et al., 2015). LDA's computing requirements are also relatively small, which makes it possible to evaluate hundreds of thousands, if not millions, of documents without difficulty under the "bag of words" metaphor (Blei et al., 2003). However, LDA is limited in that the algorithm is not pre-trained on human language; as such, topics derived in the LDA model may be repetitive and/or redundant despite these topics representing core components of a corpus. By extension, S-BERT (and the larger BERT family) are supervised algorithms trained on human language (Ettinger, 2020). Leveraging contextual embeddings (by running text against BERT, S-BERT, or BERT topic and a pipeline of processing and clustering analyses) can capture the contextual nature of documents in a corpus. As such, topics derived from S-BERT analyses are typically more clear, less redundant, and more easily contextualized regarding content in the corpus (Edinger et al., 2022). However, calculating S-BERT or BERT vectors for a series of documents in a corpus requires substantial processing power, thereby limiting the number of documents for which sentence embeddings can be captured. In a contrast between various classes of topic models, Grootendorst (2022) supports the combined use of multiple topic model approaches based on research questions or anticipated outcomes. Other studies have used a similar split structure (i.e., two different approaches with the same or similar data), further supporting our use of LDA and S-BERT topic models (Bathina et al., 2021a, b;Valdez et al., 2020).

sections.header_raw: S-BERT Overall Topics by Corpus (Original Tweets)
sections.header_norm: s bert overall topics by corpus original tweets
paragraphs: Our first BERT family analysis involved analyzing all original tweets in both corpora and extracting S-BERT derived topics per corpus. Table 2 outlines the top ten topics identified by this analysis, which reflect pockets of conversation embedded within the leak and overturn corpora. When contrasted with topics derived from LDA, we observed that, with retweets removed and a more refined algorithm, the content extracted per topic was clearer. For example, while many of the extracted topics in both corpora were against the SCOTUS' decision in Dobbs v. Jackson, we observed myriad contexts in which this was expressed. This includes claims that overturning Roe v. Wade was a sad day for women, expressing outrage at the decision by citing polls indicating 70% of Americans support upholding Roe v. Wade, and calling on politicians to codify Roe v. Wade into law. Although content was more distinct in this corpus, we also maintained high degrees of overlap between topics generated by the LDA algorithm. This may imply that retweeted content (LDA analysis) was highly similar to original content (BERT). Top ten topics per corpus also revealed only one anti-abortion/pro-SCOTUS topic, again reflecting that discourse related to the Dobbs v. Jackson decision on Twitter during this time period was primarily against SCOTUS' actions. Note, this does not imply that no anti-abortion content existed in either corpus. Rather, the most prominent or salient aspects of the themes that emerged were supportive of abortion.

sections.header_raw: S-BERT Topics Over Time for Leak and Overturn Corpora (Original Tweets)
sections.header_norm: s bert topics over time for leak and overturn corpora original tweets
paragraphs: Through an S-BERT analysis of original tweets (and all retweets removed), we had the ability to track topic prominence over time (see Fig. 2). At the onset of data collection for the leak and overturn corpora, common topics reflected breaking news cycles, namely about abortion and changes to abortion policy. Over time, topic prominence changed to reflect a focus on women's rights and healthcare. We also observed sharp SCOTUS critiques emerging as central topics throughout data collection from both corpora, but with particularly more outrage following the actual decision announcement. Over time, topic prominence consistently decreased, reflecting decreased tweet volume and similar distribution of topics during later days of data collection.

Red signifies anti-abortion content; green signifies pro-abortion content; yellow signifies unclear or uncertain abortion perpsectives sentiment analysis tool, where it was scored from -0.99 (high negativity) to 0.99 (high positivity) based on words and patterns of words within each tweet. Our sentiment analysis findings revealed highly negative affect across both corpora. However, content was generally more negative following Roe v. Wade being overturn, which may reflect a difference in the possibility of Roe v. Wade being overturned (leak corpus); and the reality that Roe v. Wade was overturned (overturn corpus). Recurring themes for positive, negative, and neutral tweets included "saving babies" for positive tweets, "news sharing" for neutral tweets, and "SCOTUS outrage" for negative tweets-though we acknowledge these frames are not representative of the entirety of both corpora. Negative tweets briefly reviewed by members of this research team (n = 500) revealed that negative language was primarily directed at SCOTUS and the Dobbs v. Jackson decision. Of note, neutrality in these tweets reflects the absence of emotion-driven language, not a neutral opinion about abortion or SCOTUS.

paragraphs: Our principal finding revealed strong reactions, many of which expressed outrage directed at condemning SCOTUS' actions and postulating a world without legal abortion. Others contained clear support for SCOTUS and the Dobbs v. Jackson decision. Yet embedded within these findings lie several important observations that warrant further discussion.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
sections.header_raw: Social Media Users Largely Indicated Surprise and Shock About Sudden SCOTUS Decision
sections.header_norm: social media users largely indicated surprise and shock about sudden scotus decision
paragraphs: Our results suggest that social media reactions amid the Politico leak and subsequent Roe v. Wade overturn were generally in support of constitutional protection for legal abortion and maintaining national legal abortion, to some extent. For both corpora, we saw numerous topics condemning SCOTUS' actions, which were further reinforced by high degrees of negative affect identified by our sentiment analysis (e.g., TWEET: OMG I cannot believe SCOTUS would do this to us; what a joke of a nation). Importantly, we also observed a substantial body of tweets framing changes to abortion legality as a direct attack on women and bodily autonomy (e.g., TWEET: So, I guess we are going back to the Victorian era treatment of women in the US)-topics that seemed to dominate discourse in both corpus over time. Social media users tweeting about abortion also tied the leak and decision to social issues emergent at the time such as Republicans protesting vaccine mandates (as an invasion of privacy) or voicing concern that other decisions regarding social issues could be similarly overturned by SCOTUS in subsequent sessions. Smaller proportions of our data indicated people celebrating the Roe v. Wade overturn (e.g., TWEET: WE are going to save millions of babies!). Yet relative to other topics in our S-BERT model, celebratory tweets never reached as great a prominence in our data as topics condemning SCOTUS' actions, again suggesting strong unpopularity of the SCOTUS' decision among Twitter users who tweeted about abortion during this timeframe.

Our findings align with findings from national polls indicating support for upholding Roe v. Wade and abortion legality more generally (Bowman & Goldstein, 2021;Jozkowski et al., 2020). Yet despite support for upholding Roe v. Wade, large pluralities of the US population's views on abortion are not unilateral or polar but more nuanced and complex (Bruce, 2020;Crawford et al., 2021;Jozkowski et al., 2021). For example, some may only endorse legal abortion if a pregnancy was a result of rape; likewise, some who broadly endorse legal abortion or believe someone should be able to have an abortion may not endorse abortion for certain reasons (Hans & Kimberly, 2014;Jozkowski et al., 2018). We observed pockets of tweets indicative of such complexity and thus criticizing the decision (e.g., TWEET: There are far too many variables in pregnancies for them to be legislated as a one-size-fits-all anti-abortion position. Do better).

Our findings may also reinforce strong evidence that abortion attitudes are more nuanced than the pro/anti dichotomy which is often portrayed in media (Jozkowski et al., 2018;Jozkowski et al., 2021). Yet, the added outrage embedded in our findings also suggests that perhaps SCOTUS' actions were so unpopular among many active on social media that even people with mixed or uncertain abortion views were willing to voice their discord via social media during this historic event. Indeed, according to at least some of our topics/tweets, at least some people advocated in response to SCOTUS' action to codify abortion as a protected right than turn abortion back to states. Although, ascertaining specific personal dispositions to abortion fall beyond the scope of this study, we also could potentially interpret the immediate shock and outrage as a direct and natural consequence of 24-h news cycles regardless of topic, though more research testing the association between media exposure and immediate reactions is needed.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
sections.header_raw: Declining Social Media Reactions Surrounding Roe v. Wade Might Be Attributed to Shifting News Cycles and Incidental News Engagement
sections.header_norm: declining social media reactions surrounding roe v wade might be attributed to shifting news cycles and incidental news engagement
paragraphs: During the immediate aftermath of the leak and subsequent Roe v. Wade overturn, internet searches for abortion rose as much as 162%, according to reports (Poliak et al., 2022) and Google Trends data. In tandem with increased internet searches for abortion, we observed similarly voluminous increases in social media reactions associated with abortion and news cycles covering Roe v. Wade. These reactions are consistent with responses to previous social issue/public health challenges (e.g., COVID-19 pandemic (Valdez et al., 2020)), gun control (Patton et al., 2019), and police violence (Bathina et al., 2021b). Yet, further discussion of engagement over time is equally warranted.

Across both leak and overturn corpora, we observed an approximate 45% decline in daily content until tweet levels stabilized approximately 1 week later. We offer two contrasting explanations for declining engagement over time, which include declining engagement as a side-effect of passive news consumption and potentially disinterest in the topic. As mentioned, social media as a news reacting tool has created a new culture regarding information sharing. Due to a barrage of news and incidental news consumption, people instinctively react to a current events cycle without internalizing larger implications behind the news cycle. Regarding abortion, it is likely that some social media users, with little knowledge of abortion legality but familiarity of Roe v. Wade, may have instinctively reacted to the Politico leak and subsequent revocation to the extent that they understand the topic (e.g., TWEET: All I know is that Roe made abortion legal and now it's gone). Indeed, given that most social media users are not activists entirely immersed in the topic, they may not have the inclination to continuously post about this shift in abortion access beyond its time as a major news cycle. Numerous studies have observed a similar outcome regarding shifting news cycles, national events, and social media users' difficulty remaining engaged with previous news cycles.

However, for some social media users, declining engagement may indicate only passive interest or disinterest in abortion rights and access for reasons that may include gender differences or political values. Indeed, demographic estimates of social media users typically skew younger, whiter, male, and more educated than the general population. For some men, abortion may not be of any interest beyond sharing information about the SCOTUS decision: (TWEET: As a guy, it doesn't really affect me but still shocking). For people whose political views do not prioritize reproductive rights, we may also observe passive interest in the topic beyond sharing a news story or offering only a brief comment. Regardless of interest or disinterest in the topic, we maintain that the ability to track posting volume over time sheds a unique perspective on abortion beliefs pertaining to social media users who are at least passively engaged in news and current events-namely that lay social media users may not be inclined to keep posting about a topic after its media exposure expires.

We also stress that decreased posting volume over time does not reflect complacency or disinterest across all social media users. People who are politically or civically engaged with abortion likely never stopped posting about abortion at the conclusion of our data collection. However, the decline of abortion-related Twitter content following the overturn is still noteworthy in that, for a brief amount of time, the shock and aftermath of the Dobbs v. Jackson decision galvanized social media users around an unprecedented topic. That said, notable political events do not appear to inspire abortionrelated political engagement even beyond posting on social media (Jozkowski et al., 2023a, b). More work should be undertaken to understand abortion activism post-Roe v. Wade being overturned, including examining over time how the Dobbs v. Jackson decision affects abortion beliefs and attitudes. Preliminary findings suggest minimal differences in attitudes among US adults when comparing immediately before and after the decision announcement (Jozkowski et al., 2023a). However, continued investigation in this regard would be fruitful as states continue to pass legislation criminalizing abortion throughout pregnancy and/or at earlier weeks' gestation (Guttmacher Institute, 2023). Still, the actions by SCOTUS represented a fundamental shift in judicial review, deviating from previous interpretations of the US Constitution-an action that had not been attempted since the early twentieth century.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
sections.header_raw: Implications for SCOTUS' Policy Decisions in the Rapid News-Cycle Era
sections.header_norm: implications for scotus policy decisions in the rapid news cycle era
paragraphs: The decision in Roe v. Wade established abortion before viability as a constitutionally protected right (Watson, 2018). Yet, Roe v. Wade was not decided on the permissibility of abortion by the US Constitution, but infringement of privacy amid targeted regulations against women seeking abortions in Texas (Garrow, 2015). Indeed, Justice Blackmun opined in the Roe v. Wade decision in 1973 that criminalizing abortion (under Texas statutes) violated a women's right to privacy as an implicit liberty guaranteed under the due process clause of the 14th Amendment to the US Constitution (Cohen et al., 2022). Roe v. Wade, which was decided on privacy, subsequently influenced national healthcare and policy for nearly five decades, emphasizing bodily autonomy and abortion's role in national healthcare. Beyond abortion-a host of other social issues germane to public interest and health were decided by SCOTUS before and after Roe v. Wade under a similar right to privacy argument-including Loving v. Virginia (interracial marriage legality), Griswold v. Connecticut (right to buy and use contraception without restriction), Lawrence v. Texas (decriminalization of same-sex sexual activity), and Obergefell v. Hodges (same sex marriage legality) (Bench-Capon, 2011). However, with Roe v. Wade effectively overturned by SCOTUS, thereby setting a new precedent for privacy-related decisions, speculation mounts on if and when SCOTUS will make similar inroads for other cases.

Justice Clarence Thomas further opined that any social issue decided on the right to privacy as an implicit liberty guarantee should be further reviewed by the court. Speculation that SCOTUS could act on one (or all) of these social issues in coming terms was directly reflected in our findings, which revealed topics specific to many social issues perceived as targeted by SCOTUS, including other cases decided on the grounds of privacy. Concern for other social issues-beyond abortion-is appropriate given that Justices Barrett, Kavanaugh, and Gorsuch (SCOTUS justices confirmed during the Trump administration) indicated Roe was settled during their respective confirmation hearings ("What Conservative Justices Said about Roe at Their Confirmation Hearings," 2022). Our findings suggest that despite an initial significant increase in negative social media sentiment toward the SCOTUS' decision, American social media users may simply shift focus, only remaining passively engaged thereafter. Therefore, while we can argue that outrage expressed on social media at Roe v. Wade's overturn was indeed sincere, for many the decision may be of only passive interest (i.e., declining tweets over time in social media activity).
sections.text:
The decision in Roe v. Wade established abortion before viability as a constitutionally protected right (Watson, 2018). Yet, Roe v. Wade was not decided on the permissibility of abortion by the US Constitution, but infringement of privacy amid targeted regulations against women seeking abortions in Texas (Garrow, 2015). Indeed, Justice Blackmun opined in the Roe v. Wade decision in 1973 that criminalizing abortion (under Texas statutes) violated a women's right to privacy as an implicit liberty guaranteed under the due process clause of the 14th Amendment to the US Constitution (Cohen et al., 2022). Roe v. Wade, which was decided on privacy, subsequently influenced national healthcare and policy for nearly five decades, emphasizing bodily autonomy and abortion's role in national healthcare. Beyond abortion-a host of other social issues germane to public interest and health were decided by SCOTUS before and after Roe v. Wade under a similar right to privacy argument-including Loving v. Virginia (interracial marriage legality), Griswold v. Connecticut (right to buy and use contraception without restriction), Lawrence v. Texas (decriminalization of same-sex sexual activity), and Obergefell v. Hodges (same sex marriage legality) (Bench-Capon, 2011). However, with Roe v. Wade effectively overturned by SCOTUS, thereby setting a new precedent for privacy-related decisions, speculation mounts on if and when SCOTUS will make similar inroads for other cases.

Justice Clarence Thomas further opined that any social issue decided on the right to privacy as an implicit liberty guarantee should be further reviewed by the court. Speculation that SCOTUS could act on one (or all) of these social issues in coming terms was directly reflected in our findings, which revealed topics specific to many social issues perceived as targeted by SCOTUS, including other cases decided on the grounds of privacy. Concern for other social issues-beyond abortion-is appropriate given that Justices Barrett, Kavanaugh, and Gorsuch (SCOTUS justices confirmed during the Trump administration) indicated Roe was settled during their respective confirmation hearings ("What Conservative Justices Said about Roe at Their Confirmation Hearings," 2022). Our findings suggest that despite an initial significant increase in negative social media sentiment toward the SCOTUS' decision, American social media users may simply shift focus, only remaining passively engaged thereafter. Wade's overturn was indeed sincere, for many the decision may be of only passive interest (i.e., declining tweets over time in social media activity).

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
paragraphs: This work is subject to limitations. First, Twitter's API limits users to an approximate 1% of tweet volume for a given query. This implies that our sample of N = 4,100,000 tweets represent a small portion of the discussion and social media activity. However, because 1% of tweets were procured at random, we believe our findings are somewhat representative of the general Twitter discourse on this topic. Second, due to processing power limitations, we were only able to download truncated tweets that contained the first 200 characters (of 280). Some tweets were inevitably not downloaded in their entirety. Third, we acknowledge a likely bias among social media users, namely that the average user is younger, skews male, and more educated than the general population (Gore et al., 2015;Padilla et al., 2018). Thus, while our findings may represent Twitter users' sentiments, we cannot claim them to be representative of the US population. Fourth, given difficulty programming a streamer, we were unable to capture tweets on the actual day of the unanticipated Politico document leak on May 2, 2022; however, we were able to capture tweets thereafter. Fifth, although we attempted to only include tweets from the USA, it is highly likely English tweets originating outside the US were included for analysis. bot analysis of a random sample of N = 5,000 tweets revealed a mean bot score of 0.51, indicating a mix of bot-authored and human authored content. Although we report interpretations of themes across corpora, we were unable to perform a deeper qualitative analysis due to the volume of data. However, we strongly encourage the use of subsets within our data for future qualitative research studies. Despite these limitations, we maintain our findings provide a clear portrait of social reactions to changes in abortion legality in the US among English-speaking Twitter users. Finally, we acknowledge that querying for Dobbs v. Jackson may have bolstered our findings. However, we maintain that given the volume of our data and national significance of Roe v. Wade, our findings remain representative of social media users' reactions to the overturn of this decision.

sections.paper_id: Valdez et al. - 2024 - Online Social Media Reactions to the Overturn of Roe v. Wade Public Health Implications and Policy
paragraphs: Despite Roe v. Wade's nearly 50-year precedent establishing abortion before viability as constitutionally protected, the legality of abortion is again within the purview of individual states. The resulting changes to the abortion policy landscape in the US are, and will continue to be, significant and carry important implications for public health. As a result, some people may seek abortion through alternative means, some of which may be unsafe, resulting in health complications for pregnant people, including death. Additionally, the discrepancy in who will be able to access abortion in terms of socio-economic status and state of residence has and will continue to increase. Furthermore, other social issues decided on the same grounds as Roe v. Wade could face similar outcomes. Therefore, public health researchers, practitioners, and the general public should engage and remain engaged in these decisions. However, prolonged engagement in issues is seemingly uncommon and may result in a return to the status quo, even if that status quo is altered.
Funding There is no funding associated with this study.
